text: The surgeon begins medial dissection of the left colon, identifying the root of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). Dissection proceeds towards the IMA root while preserving the sympathetic nerves along the aorta. Meticulous dissection around the IMA is performed, followed by its identification and ligation.

The surgeon continues medial dissection superiorly towards the splenic flexure, identifying and ligating the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV). Dissection proceeds along the pancreatic border, revealing the tail of the pancreas, until the lesser sac is opened.

The surgeon initiates superior dissection of the left colon by opening the omentum and continues dissection along the omentum towards the splenic pole. Following medial and superior mobilization, lateral mobilization of the left colon is performed, starting from the iliac fossa and progressing upwards towards the spleen. The splenic flexure and left colon are completely mobilized.
The surgeon performs additional pelvic dissection to ensure adequate distal margins, followed by the transection of the rectum.
The surgeon continues lateral dissection along the plane between the retroperitoneal fascia and parietal peritoneum, completing the separation of the pancreas and duodenum from the mesocolon.
Following lateral dissection, the surgeon performs medial dissection of the right colon, identifying the superior mesenteric and ileocolic vessels. The ileocolic vessels are then ligated.

The surgeon continues superior medial dissection after ligating the ileocolic vessels, identifying and ligating the middle colic artery and vein, gastrocolic trunk, ASPDV, and accessory right colic vein before the gastroepiploic confluence. The dissection progresses toward the lesser sac, separating the mesocolon from the pancreas head and neck, with the lesser sac opened and the pancreas head visibly separated from the mesocolon.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the right colon by performing superior dissection along the omental opening, following the previous mobilization of the left colon. The total colonic mobilization is visually achieved.

The surgeon extracts the specimen through a mini laparotomy by extending the umbilical port. The mesentery alignment is checked to prevent internal hernia or kinking of the small bowel. An end-to-end iliorectal anastomosis is performed using a circular stapler, followed by air insufflation through the anus to check for leaks. A Jackson Pratt drain is inserted posteriorly to the anastomosis site.
